claim: Sprout Social's 2026 Social Media Content Strategy Report says consumers want brands to make human-generated content their top priority.
notes: Used as broader social-content context, not as a direct employee-generated content performance study.

claim: LinkedIn and Edelman reported that 55% of hidden buyers use thought leadership during vendor evaluation.
notes: Directly supports the buyer-research rationale for useful expertise.

claim: LinkedIn and Edelman reported that 63% of hidden buyers spend more than an hour per week consuming thought leadership.
location: Why Human Expertise Matters In B2B Content

claim: The Edelman and LinkedIn 2025 B2B Thought Leadership Impact Report says 71% of hidden decision-makers view strong thought leadership as more effective than conventional marketing or sales materials at showing potential value.
location: Why Human Expertise Matters In B2B Content
notes: Used as thought-leadership context, not as a claim about employee-generated content alone.

claim: Clay says user-generated content is one of its major growth drivers.
notes: Used as a related content-loop example.

claim: Clay describes a loop where users share outputs they created and gain recognition for the work.
notes: Used to illustrate shareable practitioner output.

claim: Lovable says it grew from an open-source project to $10M ARR in two months, with community and content playing a key role.
notes: Company self-report used as a named public example.

claim: Lovable maintains a public video library with tutorials, product demonstrations, user stories, and build walkthroughs.
notes: Used as a public example library, not as performance proof.

claim: GitLab describes its handbook as the central repository for how the company runs, with more than 2,000 pages of text and an open contribution model.
notes: Used as a public documentation example.

claim: Employee-generated content should teach through real employee expertise rather than repackaging company announcements.
Employee advocacy usually distributes company-approved content through employee accounts, while employee-generated content starts with the employee's own perspective or work product.
location: What Counts As Employee-Generated Content and FAQ
notes: LinkedIn supports the advocacy definition; editorial POV supplies the category distinction.

claim: Employee-generated content should be measured by usefulness signals such as saves, comments, qualified replies, source-linked traffic, recurring questions, and answer-engine visibility, not impressions alone.
Google Search Central's helpful content guidance asks publishers to show first-hand expertise, add original value, and leave readers feeling they learned enough to achieve their goal.
location: What Good Employee-Generated Content Has In Common
notes: Used as a quality bar, not an employee-generated content case study.

claim: Shopify Engineering describes context sharing as a habit that helps developers understand the why, what, and how behind technical choices.
location: How To Turn Employee Expertise Into Useful Content
notes: Used as an example of expertise capture and context sharing.

claim: Shopify Engineering's resilient payment systems article turns years of payments infrastructure lessons into practical tips for other builders.
location: How To Turn Employee Expertise Into Useful Content

claim: LinkedIn's employee advocacy guidance emphasizes sustainable, organic, voluntary participation from employees who have a real reason to participate.
notes: Supports the point that not every employee should be forced into publishing.
